question_title: Will the International Criminal Court open a formal investigation into alleged crimes linked to the 2026 Iran conflict before May 1, 2026?
summary: The ICC opening a formal investigation into the 2026 Iran conflict before May 1, 2026 faces near-insurmountable barriers. The fundamental obstacle is jurisdiction: Iran, the US, and Israel are all non-parties to the Rome Statute. Iran has not filed an Article 12(3) declaration granting ICC jurisdiction despite being urged to do so. A UNSC referral is blocked by the US veto. No state party (e.g., Jordan, which was struck by Iranian missiles) has referred the situation. The ICC situations page currently lists no Iran investigation. The ICC prosecutor's office is in institutional crisis with Karim Khan on leave facing disciplinary proceedings. Legal experts unanimously say there is "no path" to ICC jurisdiction. Even if jurisdiction were somehow established today, only ~20 days remain, and the fastest ICC investigation ever (Ukraine) required pre-existing jurisdictional arrangements. The 3% accounts for the small residual possibility of a sudden jurisdictional development.

factors.description: Fundamental jurisdictional barrier: Iran, US, and Israel are all non-parties to the Rome Statute. ICC cannot investigate without jurisdiction via state party territory/nationals, UNSC referral, or Article 12(3) declaration.
Iran has NOT filed an Article 12(3) declaration granting ICC jurisdiction despite being urged to do so by DAWN in March 2026. No evidence any other affected state party has referred the situation.
US holds UNSC veto, making a Security Council referral to the ICC essentially impossible.
ICC chief prosecutor Karim Khan has been on voluntary leave since May 2025 and faces disciplinary proceedings — the office is in institutional crisis.
ICC situations page as of today shows NO Iran-related investigation. No press release or court filing announcing a formal investigation has been found.
Only ~20 days remain before May 1, 2026 deadline. Even the fastest ICC investigation (Ukraine 2022) required pre-existing jurisdictional arrangements and exceptional circumstances.
Legal experts unanimously say there is 'no path to the ICC' for this conflict, confirming the jurisdictional analysis.
Jordan (ICC member) was struck by Iranian missiles — theoretical jurisdictional pathway exists if Jordan referred the situation, but no evidence of this happening.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% probability (logit: -3.5)**

The ICC is extremely unlikely to open a formal investigation into the 2026 Iran conflict before May 1, 2026. The core reasons:

1. **No jurisdiction**: Iran, the US, and Israel are all non-parties to the Rome Statute. The ICC simply cannot investigate without a legal basis.
2. **No jurisdictional pathway has been activated**: Iran hasn't filed an Article 12(3) declaration, no state party has referred the situation, and the US blocks any UNSC referral via veto.
3. **No investigation exists today**: The ICC situations page lists no Iran-related investigation, and no announcement has been found.
4. **ICC institutional crisis**: Chief prosecutor Khan has been on leave since May 2025 and faces disciplinary proceedings.
5. **Only ~20 days remain**: Even the fastest-ever ICC investigation (Ukraine) required pre-existing jurisdictional arrangements that don't exist here.
6. **Expert consensus**: Legal experts say there is "no path" to ICC jurisdiction over this conflict.

The 3% residual accounts for the unlikely scenario where Iran or another affected state suddenly creates a jurisdictional basis AND the ICC acts with unprecedented speed.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the International Criminal Court open a formal investigation into alleged crimes linked to the 2026 Iran conflict before May 1, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43129)
[ICC Prosecutor Says Trump’s Threats to Target Iranian Energy Infrastructure Could Constitute War Crimes | News | The Harvard Crimson](https://www.thecrimson.com/article/2026/4/7/ocampo-forum-iran/)
[Experts Say US-Iran Dispute Won’t Land in International Court](https://www.courthousenews.com/experts-say-us-iran-dispute-wont-land-in-international-court/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if, before May 1, 2026, the Office of the Prosecutor or the Court announces that it has opened a formal investigation into alleged crimes linked to the 2026 Iran conflict. This question resolves to no otherwise. The primary source is the International Criminal Court situations page at <https://www.icc-cpi.int/situations-under-investigations> where the Court lists situations under investigation, and if the investigation is first announced through a press release or court filing, the ICC news page at <https://www.icc-cpi.int/news> may be used to identify it. (icc-cpi.int)
fine_print: A preliminary examination, a statement of concern, evidence-gathering language, or general monitoring does not resolve yes. Arrest warrants do not resolve yes unless the ICC clearly links them to a formal investigation into alleged crimes tied to the 2026 Iran conflict. Conflict naming variants count if the ICC clearly refers to the same 2026 hostilities. Candidate Source: the ICC cases page at <https://www.icc-cpi.int/cases> may be used if a related case is opened first. If the primary source is unavailable or there is a dispute, resolve using credible sources per <https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions>
